grades.justification: Catches the two most important real problems: the caller never supplies `priority`/`conductor_approved`, and the lane tie-break is only first-code-point. It also notices real mirror divergences such as failed/skipped deps and invalid-dependency handling. The main weakness is judgment/precision: it overstates the P1-4 result as not deterministic, when the new path is at least more deterministic than before for a fixed task order, and it treats some mirror differences more like shipping defects than behavior-preserving divergences.

Strong, high-signal review. It verifies the central end-to-end bug correctly, explains why the claimed ranking policy is dead code in practice, and accurately calls out the first-character tie-break and the client-only nature of the P1-4 mitigation. It still leans a bit hard on the failed/skipped dependency divergence as a major issue even though that behavior matches the removed inline filter, and it misses some secondary concerns, but overall it is precise and well judged.

Finds the most important issue around dropped ranking metadata and correctly notes the first-character tie-break, but it is internally inconsistent about mirror faithfulness: it flags the failed/skipped dependency divergence as a finding, then later says `selectReadyTasks` is semantically faithful to `getReadyEntries`, which is not true. That contradiction hurts both precision and judgment, and the coverage is only moderate beyond the main P1-4 path.

Best review of the set. It identifies the key end-to-end defect, explains the practical effect of the first-code-point ranking bug, distinguishes real regressions from behavior-preserving divergences, and gives the clearest mirror-faithfulness analysis. It also has the strongest verdict/judgment around this being a mitigation rather than the authoritative P1-4 fix. A few lower-priority findings are somewhat speculative or edge-casey, so it is not perfect on precision, but it is still the strongest overall.

Good coverage of the major themes: missing ranking inputs, first-character tie-break, mirror divergence, and the client-side-only nature of the P1-4 change. It also gives a balanced verdict. However, it adds weaker findings like lack of tests and a MAESTRO_STATE note that are not concrete code defects in this commit, which lowers precision, and some of its mirror commentary blurs adaptation with true semantic equivalence.
